sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Each action moves the white character 3 cells in that direction. - White char now at rows 14-28, cols 14-28. Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check the green count after DOWN + SPACE. I need to track the pattern more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green total: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in greens)} in {len(greens)} pieces")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green went from 54 to 108 - it increased! So DOWN + SPACE increased green this time. This contradicts my earlier pattern. Let me reconsider.
Let me track the history more carefully:
- 144 -> 135 (UP, reduced by 9)
- 135 -> 99 (UP, reduced by 36)
- 99 -> 54 (UP, reduced by 45)
- 54 -> 90 (DOWN, increased by 36)
- 90 -> 99 (DOWN, increased by 9)
- 99 -> 72 (DOWN, reduced by 27)
- 72 -> 90 (DOWN, increased by 18)
- 90 -> 45 (SPACE, reduced by 45)
- 45 -> 90 (SPACE, increased by 45)
- 90 -> 72 (UP, UP, SPACE, reduced by 18)
- 72 -> 108 (SPACE, increased by 36)
- 108 -> 54 (DOWN, DOWN, SPACE, reduced by 54)
- 54 -> 90 (UP, SPACE, increased by 36)
- 90 -> 45 (DOWN, SPACE, reduced by 45)
- 45 -> 54 (UP, increased by 9)
- 54 -> 108 (DOWN, SPACE, increased by 54)
So the pattern isn't consistent. Sometimes DOWN + SPACE reduces, sometimes it increases. What's different?
Let me think about the character's orientation. The character seems to rotate when SPACE is pressed. Maybe the effect depends on the character's orientation relative to the green pieces.
Looking at the images:
1. Character upright (red on top): SPACE reduces green
2. Character rotated 90° (red on right): SPACE increases green
3. Character inverted (red on bottom): SPACE reduces green
4. Character rotated 270° (red on left): SPACE increases green
Or maybe it's the opposite. Let me track the orientation more carefully.
Actually, let me look at the red block positions:
- Current: rows 35-37, 53-55 and rows 41-43, 53-55 (right side)
- Previous: rows 29-31, 41-43 and 47-49 (top) and rows 35-37, 38-40 (left)
The character rotates 90° clockwise each time SPACE is pressed:
- Red on top -> red on right -> red on bottom -> red on left -> repeat
Let me check: when red is on top, SPACE reduces green. When red is on right, SPACE increases green. When red is on bottom, SPACE reduces green. When red is on left, SPACE increases green.
So the pattern is: SPACE reduces green when red is on top or bottom (vertical), and increases when red is on left or right (horizontal).
Current state: red is on the right (rows 35-37, 53-55 and rows 41-43, 53-55). So SPACE would increase green. I need to rotate the character so red is on top or bottom before pressing SPACE.
But wait, I just pressed SPACE and green increased. So the current orientation (red on right) causes SPACE to increase green. If I press SPACE again, the character rotates to red on bottom, and then the NEXT SPACE would reduce green.
Let me try: SPACE (rotates to red on bottom), then move, then SPACE (should reduce).
Actually, let me reconsider. The pattern might be:
- After SPACE, the character rotates. The NEXT SPACE's effect depends on the new orientation.
- When red is vertical (top/bottom), SPACE reduces green.
- When red is horizontal (left/right), SPACE increases green.
So if I press SPACE now (red on right -> rotates to red on bottom), then the next SPACE would reduce green (since red is on bottom).
Let me try: SPACE, SPACE (first rotates to bottom, second reduces green).
Wait, but I need to also move the character to overlap with green pieces. Let me think...
Actually, maybe I should just try SPACE now to rotate the character, then check the green count. If the pattern holds, SPACE with red on right should increase green (which we just saw: 54 -> 108). Then if I press SPACE again, red rotates to bottom, and the next SPACE should reduce green.
But I already pressed SPACE and green went from 54 to 108. So the character is now in a new orientation. Let me check where the red blocks are now.
From the segmentation: red is at rows 35-37, 53-55 and rows 41-43, 53-55. That's on the right side. So after the SPACE, red is still on the right? That doesn't make sense if SPACE rotates the character.
Wait, let me re-read the segmentation. The red blocks are at:
- rows 35-37, cols 53-55
- rows 41-43, cols 53-55
And the white character is at rows 32-46, cols 38-52. So the red blocks are to the RIGHT of the character. This means the character is facing right (or has red on the right side).
Before the SPACE, the red blocks were at:
- rows 29-31, cols 41-43 and 47-49 (top)
- rows 35-37, cols 38-40 (left)
So before SPACE, red was on top and left. After SPACE, red is on the right. The character rotated.
Hmm, the pattern is getting complicated. Let me just try pressing SPACE again and see what happens to green.

sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green went from 54 to 108 - DOWN+SPACE increased it
# Let me track the pattern. The character rotates with SPACE.
# Let me try SPACE alone to see the effect
action(['SPACE'], intent='solve')
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
